subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Electron Emission
text: Metal Conductivity Metals possess free electrons, which contribute to their ability to conduct electricity. These electrons are negatively charged particles.
text_html:
Metal Conductivity
Metals possess free electrons, which contribute to their ability to conduct electricity. These electrons are negatively charged particles.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Electron Emission
text: Electron Escape from Metal Free electrons are confined within metals due to the attractive forces from ions. They need sufficient energy to break free from these forces.
text_html:
Electron Escape from Metal
Free electrons are confined within metals due to the attractive forces from ions. They need sufficient energy to break free from these forces.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Electron Emission
text: Work Function The term for the minimum energy required by an electron to escape the metal surface is the work function, denoted by {tex} \phi_0 {/tex} .
text_html:
Work Function
The term for the minimum energy required by an electron to escape the metal surface is the work function, denoted by {tex} \phi_0 {/tex}.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Electron Emission
text: Measurement of Work Function Work function is often measured in electron volts (eV). 1 eV equals the energy gained by an electron accelerated through a potential difference of 1 volt.
text_html:
Measurement of Work Function
Work function is often measured in electron volts (eV). 1 eV equals the energy gained by an electron accelerated through a potential difference of 1 volt.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Electron Emission
text: Energy Unit: Electron Volt 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ joules. This is a commonly used unit in atomic and nuclear physics.
text_html:
Energy Unit: Electron Volt
1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ joules. This is a commonly used unit in atomic and nuclear physics.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Electron Emission
text: Factors Influencing Work Function The work function {tex} (\phi_0) {/tex} varies based on metal properties and the surface characteristics of the metal.
text_html:
Factors Influencing Work Function
The work function {tex} (\phi_0) {/tex} varies based on metal properties and the surface characteristics of the metal.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Electron Emission
text: Processes Supplying Energy for Electron Emission Thermionic Emission : Heating provides the needed thermal energy for electrons to escape. Field Emission : Applying a strong electric field pulls electrons from the metal. Photoelectric Emission : Light absorbed by the metal releases electrons (photoelectrons).
text_html:
Processes Supplying Energy for Electron Emission
  • Thermionic Emission: Heating provides the needed thermal energy for electrons to escape.
  • Field Emission: Applying a strong electric field pulls electrons from the metal.
  • Photoelectric Emission: Light absorbed by the metal releases electrons (photoelectrons).

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Electron Emission
text: Thermionic Emission Through appropriate heating, thermal energy is transferred to free electrons, enabling them to leave the metal surface.
text_html:
Thermionic Emission
Through appropriate heating, thermal energy is transferred to free electrons, enabling them to leave the metal surface.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Electron Emission
text: Field Emission Electrons can be emitted from a metal by applying a strong electric field, similar to the principle used in spark plugs.
text_html:
Field Emission
Electrons can be emitted from a metal by applying a strong electric field, similar to the principle used in spark plugs.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Electron Emission
text: Photoelectric Emission When light hits a metal surface at the right frequency, it releases electrons, referred to as photoelectrons.
text_html:
Photoelectric Emission
When light hits a metal surface at the right frequency, it releases electrons, referred to as photoelectrons.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hertz’s observations
text: Work function- The minimum amount of energy required by an electron to just escape from the metal surface is known as work function of the metal.
text_html: Work function- The minimum amount of energy required by an electron to just escape from the metal surface is known as work function of the metal.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hertz’s observations
text: One Electron Volt: (1eV)-It is the kinetic energy gained by an electron when it is accelerated through a potential difference of 1 volt. 1 eV = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J The main features of photons are as follows: A photon travels with the speed of light. The rest mass of a photon is zero i.e., a photon cannot exist at rest. Energy of a photon, E = hv Momentum of a photon, p = mc Photoelectric Effect:- The phenomenon of emission of electrons from a metallic surface when light of appropriate frequency (above threshold frequency) is incident on it, known as photoelectric effect.
text_html:
One Electron Volt: (1eV)-It is the kinetic energy gained by an electron when it is accelerated through a potential difference of 1 volt.
1 eV = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19J
The main features of photons are as follows:

  1. A photon travels with the speed of light.
  2. The rest mass of a photon is zero i.e., a photon cannot exist at rest.
  3. Energy of a photon, E = hv
  4. Momentum of a photon, p = mc

Photoelectric Effect:- The phenomenon of emission of electrons from a metallic surface when light of appropriate frequency (above threshold frequency) is incident on it, known as photoelectric effect.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hertz’s observations
text: Factors which Effect Photoelectric Effect of potential on photoelectric current: It is can be shown in fig. potential v/s photoelectric current. Effect of intensity of incident radiations on photoelectric current: For frequency of radiations as constant.
text_html:
Factors which Effect Photoelectric

  • Effect of potential on photoelectric current: It is can be shown in fig. potential v/s photoelectric current.
  • Effect of intensity of incident radiations on photoelectric current: For frequency of radiations as constant.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hertz’s observations
text: Factor which Effect Photoelectric Effect of frequency of the incident radiations on stopping potential: For constant intensity.
text_html:
Factor which Effect Photoelectric

  • Effect of frequency of the incident radiations on stopping potential: For constant intensity.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hertz’s observations
text: The stopping potential V 0 depends on (i) The frequency of incident light and (ii) the nature of emitter material. For a given frequency of incident light, the stopping potential is independent of its intensity. From this graph between frequency v, stopping potential Plank's constant (h) can be determined.
text_html:
The stopping potential V0 depends on (i) The frequency of incident light and (ii) the nature of
emitter material. For a given frequency of incident light, the stopping potential is independent of its intensity.

From this graph between frequency v, stopping potential Plank's constant (h) can be determined.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: Hertz's observations The phenomenon of photoelectric emission was discovered in 1887 by Heinrich Hertz (1857-1894), during his electromagnetic wave experiments. Hertz observed that high voltage sparks across the detector loop were enhanced when the emitter plate was illuminated by ultraviolet light from an arc lamp. Light shining on the metal surface some how facilitated the escape of free, charged particles which we now know as electrons. When light falls on a metal surface, some electrons near the surface absorb enough energy from the incident radiation to overcome the attraction of the positive ions in the material of the surface. After gaining sufficient energy from the incident light, the electrons escape from the surface of the metal into the surrounding space.
text_html:
Hertz's observations
The phenomenon of photoelectric emission was discovered in 1887 by Heinrich Hertz (1857-1894), during his electromagnetic wave experiments.
Hertz observed that high voltage sparks across the detector loop were enhanced when the emitter plate was illuminated by ultraviolet light from an arc lamp. Light shining on the metal surface some how facilitated the escape of free, charged particles which we now know as electrons. When light falls on a metal surface, some electrons near the surface absorb enough energy from the incident radiation to overcome the attraction of the positive ions in the material of the surface. After gaining sufficient energy from the incident light, the electrons escape from the surface of the metal into the surrounding space.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: Hallwachs' and Lenard's observations Lenard (1862-1947) observed that when ultraviolet radiations were allowed to fall on the emitter plate of an evacuated glass tube enclosing two electrodes (metal plates), current flows in the circuit As soon as the ultraviolet radiations were stopped, the current flow also stopped. These observations indicate that when ultraviolet radiations fall on the emitter plate $C$, electrons are ejected from it which are attracted towards the positive, collector plate A by the electric field. The electrons flow through the evacuated glass tube, resulting in the current flow. Thus, light falling on the surface of the emitter causes current in the external circuit. Hallwachs and Lenard studied how this photo current varied with collector plate potential, and with frequency and intensity of incident light. Photoelectric Effect experimental setup-
text_html:
Hallwachs' and Lenard's observations
Lenard (1862-1947) observed that when ultraviolet radiations were allowed to fall on the emitter plate of an evacuated glass tube enclosing two electrodes (metal plates), current flows in the circuit As soon as the ultraviolet radiations were stopped, the current flow also stopped. These observations indicate that when ultraviolet radiations fall on the emitter plate $C$, electrons are ejected from it which are attracted towards the positive, collector plate A by the electric field. The electrons flow through the evacuated glass tube, resulting in the current flow. Thus, light falling on the surface of the emitter causes current in the external circuit. Hallwachs and Lenard studied how this photo current varied with collector plate potential, and with frequency and intensity of incident light.
Photoelectric Effect experimental setup-

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: Photoelectric Effect Investigators Wilhelm Hallwachs and Philipp Lenard explored photoelectric emission between 1886 and 1902.
text_html:
Photoelectric Effect Investigators
  • Wilhelm Hallwachs and Philipp Lenard explored photoelectric emission between 1886 and 1902.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: Lenard's Initial Observations Observed current flow when UV light hit an emitter plate in an evacuated glass tube containing electrodes. Current ceased when UV light was stopped, suggesting electron emission from the plate.
text_html:
Lenard's Initial Observations
  • Observed current flow when UV light hit an emitter plate in an evacuated glass tube containing electrodes.
  • Current ceased when UV light was stopped, suggesting electron emission from the plate.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: Mechanism of Current Flow Electrons ejected from the emitter move towards the positive collector plate due to the electric field, resulting in current flow.
text_html:
Mechanism of Current Flow
  • Electrons ejected from the emitter move towards the positive collector plate due to the electric field, resulting in current flow.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: Variations in Photoelectric Current Hallwachs and Lenard examined how potential, frequency, and intensity of light affect photo current.
text_html:
Variations in Photoelectric Current
  • Hallwachs and Lenard examined how potential, frequency, and intensity of light affect photo current.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: Hallwachs' Zinc Plate Experiments In 1888, Hallwachs noted a negatively charged zinc plate lost charge under UV light. Uncharged zinc gained positive charge when illuminated, possibly due to emission of negatively charged particles.
text_html:
Hallwachs' Zinc Plate Experiments
  • In 1888, Hallwachs noted a negatively charged zinc plate lost charge under UV light.
  • Uncharged zinc gained positive charge when illuminated, possibly due to emission of negatively charged particles.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: Discovery of Electrons and Photoemission Post 1897, known that incident light causes electron emission from emitter plates.
text_html:
Discovery of Electrons and Photoemission
  • Post 1897, known that incident light causes electron emission from emitter plates.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: Threshold Frequency No electron emission occurs when incident light has a frequency below a specific threshold, which varies by material.
text_html:
Threshold Frequency
  • No electron emission occurs when incident light has a frequency below a specific threshold, which varies by material.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: Metal Sensitivity to Light Metals like zinc, cadmium, and magnesium respond primarily to UV light for electron emission. Alkali metals (lithium, sodium, potassium, etc.) can emit electrons under visible light.
text_html:
Metal Sensitivity to Light
  • Metals like zinc, cadmium, and magnesium respond primarily to UV light for electron emission.
  • Alkali metals (lithium, sodium, potassium, etc.) can emit electrons under visible light.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: Photoelectrons After the discovery of electrons, those emitted by light were labeled photoelectrons.
text_html:
Photoelectrons
  • After the discovery of electrons, those emitted by light were labeled photoelectrons.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect - Hallwachs’ and Lenard’s observations
text: The Photoelectric Effect Phenomenon where light induces electron emission from photosensitive surfaces.
text_html:
The Photoelectric Effect
  • Phenomenon where light induces electron emission from photosensitive surfaces.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Experimental Study of Photoelectric Effect
text: Photoelectric Effect: Experimental Setup The experiment consists of an evacuated glass tube with two metal plates. A photosensitive plate C (emitter) and a collector plate A are included. Monochromatic light irradiates the photosensitive plate C, emitting electrons. Electrons are attracted to plate A, creating an electric current.
text_html:
Photoelectric Effect: Experimental Setup
  • The experiment consists of an evacuated glass tube with two metal plates.
  • A photosensitive plate C (emitter) and a collector plate A are included.
  • Monochromatic light irradiates the photosensitive plate C, emitting electrons.
  • Electrons are attracted to plate A, creating an electric current.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Experimental Study of Photoelectric Effect
text: Photoelectric Effect: Study Variations Vary intensity of radiation to observe photocurrent changes. Frequency alterations affect stopping potential. The potential difference between The plates adjusts current flow. The material of plate C impacts the emitted electrons.
text_html:
Photoelectric Effect: Study Variations
  • Vary intensity of radiation to observe photocurrent changes.
  • Frequency alterations affect stopping potential.
  • The potential difference between The plates adjusts current flow.
  • The material of plate C impacts the emitted electrons.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Experimental Study of Photoelectric Effect
text: Intensity's Impact on Photocurrent Higher light intensity increases the photocurrent. Emission of more electrons per second is noted. Saturation current rises with higher intensities.
text_html:
Intensity's Impact on Photocurrent
  • Higher light intensity increases the photocurrent.
  • Emission of more electrons per second is noted.
  • Saturation current rises with higher intensities.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Experimental Study of Photoelectric Effect
text: Effect of Potential on Current Increasing positive potential of collector plate enhances photocurrent. Saturation current is achieved when all photoelectrons are collected. Reversing polarity applies a retarding potential, reducing photocurrent.
text_html:
Effect of Potential on Current
  • Increasing positive potential of collector plate enhances photocurrent.
  • Saturation current is achieved when all photoelectrons are collected.
  • Reversing polarity applies a retarding potential, reducing photocurrent.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Experimental Study of Photoelectric Effect
text: Maximum Kinetic Energy and Stopping Potential Stopping potential is the negative potential needed to halt photoelectrons. Does not depend on light intensity. Affected by light frequency and plate material.
text_html:
Maximum Kinetic Energy and Stopping Potential
  • Stopping potential is the negative potential needed to halt photoelectrons.
  • Does not depend on light intensity.
  • Affected by light frequency and plate material.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Experimental Study of Photoelectric Effect
text: Relation Between Frequency and Stopping Potential Stopping potential increases with frequency. Graph of frequency versus stopping potential is linear. The threshold frequency is when the stopping potential is zero.
text_html:
Relation Between Frequency and Stopping Potential
  • Stopping potential increases with frequency.
  • Graph of frequency versus stopping potential is linear.
  • The threshold frequency is when the stopping potential is zero.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Experimental Study of Photoelectric Effect
text: Threshold Frequency Minimum frequency needed for photoelectric emission. No emission occurs below the threshold, regardless of light intensity.
text_html:
Threshold Frequency
  • Minimum frequency needed for photoelectric emission.
  • No emission occurs below the threshold, regardless of light intensity.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Experimental Study of Photoelectric Effect
text: Photosensitive Material Dependence Different materials have different threshold frequencies. Response varies by wavelength of incident light. Ultraviolet light can cause effects in metals like copper.
text_html:
Photosensitive Material Dependence
  • Different materials have different threshold frequencies.
  • Response varies by wavelength of incident light.
  • Ultraviolet light can cause effects in metals like copper.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Experimental Study of Photoelectric Effect
text: Instantaneous Emission Emission starts instantaneously when above threshold frequency. The process occurs within nano-seconds (10^-9s).
text_html:
Instantaneous Emission
  • Emission starts instantaneously when above threshold frequency.
  • The process occurs within nano-seconds (10^-9s).

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Experimental Study of Photoelectric Effect
text: Summary of Photoelectric Effect Observations Photoelectric current is proportional to light intensity. Saturation current proportional to intensity; stopping potential independent of it. Threshold frequency needed for emission; energy rises linearly with frequency. Emission process is rapid and immediate.
text_html:
Summary of Photoelectric Effect Observations
  1. Photoelectric current is proportional to light intensity.
  2. Saturation current proportional to intensity; stopping potential independent of it.
  3. Threshold frequency needed for emission; energy rises linearly with frequency.
  4. Emission process is rapid and immediate.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect and Wave Theory of Light
text: Wave Nature of Light Light behaves as an electromagnetic wave. This includes planes of electric and magnetic fields extended through space.
text_html:
Wave Nature of Light
Light behaves as an electromagnetic wave. This includes planes of electric and magnetic fields extended through space.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect and Wave Theory of Light
text: Interference Phenomenon Occurs when two or more waves overlap to form a composite wave with its amplitude affected by the relative phases of the overlapping waves.
text_html:
Interference Phenomenon
Occurs when two or more waves overlap to form a composite wave with its amplitude affected by the relative phases of the overlapping waves.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect and Wave Theory of Light
text: Diffraction Phenomenon This is the bending of waves around obstacles, demonstrating light's wave nature.
text_html:
Diffraction Phenomenon
This is the bending of waves around obstacles, demonstrating light's wave nature.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect and Wave Theory of Light
text: Polarisation Phenomenon Polarisation refers to the ability of waves to oscillate with more than one orientation. It is natural evidence of light as a transverse wave.
text_html:
Polarisation Phenomenon
Polarisation refers to the ability of waves to oscillate with more than one orientation. It is natural evidence of light as a transverse wave.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect and Wave Theory of Light
text: Continuous Distribution of Energy In the wave theory of light, energy is continuously spread across the wave front and absorbed by electrons at the metal's surface.
text_html:
Continuous Distribution of Energy
In the wave theory of light, energy is continuously spread across the wave front and absorbed by electrons at the metal's surface.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect and Wave Theory of Light
text: Intensity and Amplitude - Intensity affects the amplitude of electric and magnetic fields. - Higher intensity means greater energy is absorbed by electrons.
text_html:
Intensity and Amplitude
- Intensity affects the amplitude of electric and magnetic fields.
- Higher intensity means greater energy is absorbed by electrons.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect and Wave Theory of Light
text: Kinetic Energy and Intensity Increased intensity should increase the kinetic energy of photoelectrons if wave theory is correct. This assumption doesn't match observed results.
text_html:
Kinetic Energy and Intensity
Increased intensity should increase the kinetic energy of photoelectrons if wave theory is correct. This assumption doesn't match observed results.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect and Wave Theory of Light
text: Threshold Frequency Issue The wave theory predicts no threshold frequency constraint for liberating photoelectrons, which contradicts experimental data showing a critical frequency exists.
text_html:
Threshold Frequency Issue
The wave theory predicts no threshold frequency constraint for liberating photoelectrons, which contradicts experimental data showing a critical frequency exists.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect and Wave Theory of Light
text: Energy Absorption Rate Continuous energy absorption could take hours per electron, contradicting photoelectric emission's instantaneous nature.
text_html:
Energy Absorption Rate
Continuous energy absorption could take hours per electron, contradicting photoelectric emission's instantaneous nature.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Photoelectric Effect and Wave Theory of Light
text: Photoelectric Emission Incompatibility Despite its detailed predictions, the wave picture fails to explain photoelectric emission's basic observations.
text_html:
Photoelectric Emission Incompatibility
Despite its detailed predictions, the wave picture fails to explain photoelectric emission's basic observations.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
text: Einstein's Proposal on Photoelectric Effect (1905) - Einstein suggested a new idea that electromagnetic radiation consists of discrete energy units called quanta. - These quanta explain the process of photoelectric emission, dispelling the notion of continuous energy absorption.
text_html: Einstein's Proposal on Photoelectric Effect (1905) - Einstein suggested a new idea that electromagnetic radiation consists of discrete energy units called quanta. - These quanta explain the process of photoelectric emission, dispelling the notion of continuous energy absorption.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
text: Quantum of Radiant Energy - Each quantum has energy expressed as {tex} hv {/tex} , where {tex} h {/tex} represents Planck's constant and {tex} v {/tex} is the frequency. - Electrons absorb a quantum of energy ( {tex} hv {/tex} ), causing photoelectric emission if this energy exceeds the material's work function ( {tex} \phi_0 {/tex} ).
text_html: Quantum of Radiant Energy - Each quantum has energy expressed as {tex} hv {/tex}, where {tex} h {/tex} represents Planck's constant and {tex} v {/tex} is the frequency. - Electrons absorb a quantum of energy ({tex} hv {/tex}), causing photoelectric emission if this energy exceeds the material's work function ({tex} \phi_0 {/tex}).

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
text: Max Kinetic Energy and Photoelectric Equation - The emitted electron's maximum kinetic energy can be calculated using {tex} K_{\max }=hv-\phi_0 {/tex} . - Equation 11.2 links the maximum kinetic energy directly with frequency, independent of light intensity.
text_html: Max Kinetic Energy and Photoelectric Equation - The emitted electron's maximum kinetic energy can be calculated using {tex} K_{\max }=hv-\phi_0 {/tex}. - Equation 11.2 links the maximum kinetic energy directly with frequency, independent of light intensity.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
text: Threshold Frequency ( {tex} v_0 {/tex} ) - Photoelectric emission occurs only if {tex} hv>\phi_0 {/tex} or {tex} v>v_0 {/tex} . - {tex} v_0 {/tex} is the threshold frequency needed for electron emission: {tex} v_0=\frac{\phi_0}{h} {/tex} .
text_html: Threshold Frequency ({tex} v_0 {/tex}) - Photoelectric emission occurs only if {tex} hv>\phi_0 {/tex} or {tex} v>v_0 {/tex}. - {tex} v_0 {/tex} is the threshold frequency needed for electron emission: {tex} v_0=\frac{\phi_0}{h} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
text: Intensity and Photoelectric Current - Photoelectric current is proportional to light's intensity when {tex} v>v_0 {/tex} . Greater number of photons leads to more emitted electrons. Intensity does not impact emission occurrence, just its volume.
text_html:
Intensity and Photoelectric Current - Photoelectric current is proportional to light's intensity when {tex} v>v_0 {/tex}.

  • Greater number of photons leads to more emitted electrons.
  • Intensity does not impact emission occurrence, just its volume.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
text: Instantaneous Nature of Photoelectric Effect - Absorption of a light quantum by an electron causes instant emission. - Photoelectric emission is unaffected by light intensity in terms of delay or speed.
text_html: Instantaneous Nature of Photoelectric Effect - Absorption of a light quantum by an electron causes instant emission. - Photoelectric emission is unaffected by light intensity in terms of delay or speed.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
text: Equation for Stopping Potential - Using {tex} eV_0=hv-\phi_0 {/tex} , introduce the formula: {tex} V_0=\frac{h}{e}v-\frac{\phi_0}{e} {/tex} . - It shows that the relationship between stopping potential and frequency is linear, with a slope of {tex} \frac{h}{e} {/tex} .
text_html: Equation for Stopping Potential - Using {tex} eV_0=hv-\phi_0 {/tex}, introduce the formula: {tex} V_0=\frac{h}{e}v-\frac{\phi_0}{e} {/tex}. - It shows that the relationship between stopping potential and frequency is linear, with a slope of {tex} \frac{h}{e} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
text: Millikan's Experiment (1906-1916) - Despite initial skepticism, Millikan's experiments on photoelectric effect inadvertently proved Einstein's theory. - He successfully measured Planck's constant by assessing the slope of {tex} V_0 {/tex} vs {tex} v {/tex} for sodium.
text_html: Millikan's Experiment (1906-1916) - Despite initial skepticism, Millikan's experiments on photoelectric effect inadvertently proved Einstein's theory. - He successfully measured Planck's constant by assessing the slope of {tex} V_0 {/tex} vs {tex} v {/tex} for sodium.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
text: Validation Through Multiple Experiments - The alignment of theoretical and experimental results validated Einstein's photoelectric equation. - Millikan and others verified emissions across many alkali metals and varying radiation frequencies.
text_html: Validation Through Multiple Experiments - The alignment of theoretical and experimental results validated Einstein's photoelectric equation. - Millikan and others verified emissions across many alkali metals and varying radiation frequencies.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
text: Acceptance of Einstein's Photoelectric Effect - The successful explanation and empirical support led to broader acceptance of Einstein's idea. - The photoelectric effect set a precedent for understanding light and energy quanta.
text_html: Acceptance of Einstein's Photoelectric Effect - The successful explanation and empirical support led to broader acceptance of Einstein's idea. - The photoelectric effect set a precedent for understanding light and energy quanta.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
text: Photoelectric Effect Insight The photoelectric effect shows that light behaves as quanta or packets of energy when interacting with matter.
text_html:
Photoelectric Effect Insight
The photoelectric effect shows that light behaves as quanta or packets of energy when interacting with matter.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
text: Light Quantum and Momentum Einstein discovered that a light quantum has momentum, expressed as {tex} h v / c {/tex} , indicating its particle-like traits.
text_html:
Light Quantum and Momentum
Einstein discovered that a light quantum has momentum, expressed as {tex} h v / c {/tex}, indicating its particle-like traits.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
text: Photon Discovery Light quanta with specific energy and momentum were identified as particles termed photons.
text_html:
Photon Discovery
Light quanta with specific energy and momentum were identified as particles termed photons.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
text: Compton's Experiment A.H. Compton's 1924 experiment further confirmed light's particle-like behavior in X-ray scattering from electrons.
text_html:
Compton's Experiment
A.H. Compton's 1924 experiment further confirmed light's particle-like behavior in X-ray scattering from electrons.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
text: Nobel Prize Recognition Einstein received the 1921 Nobel Prize for his work in theoretical physics and the photoelectric effect, followed by Millikan in 1923 for the elementary charge and the photoelectric effect.
text_html:
Nobel Prize Recognition
Einstein received the 1921 Nobel Prize for his work in theoretical physics and the photoelectric effect, followed by Millikan in 1923 for the elementary charge and the photoelectric effect.

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
text: Photon Energy and Momentum Photons have energy {tex} E(=h v) {/tex} and momentum {tex} p(=h v / c) {/tex} , moving at the speed of light {tex} c {/tex} .
text_html:
Photon Energy and Momentum
Photons have energy {tex} E(=h v) {/tex} and momentum {tex} p(=h v / c) {/tex}, moving at the speed of light {tex} c {/tex}.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
text: Frequency and Energy All light photons of a particular frequency or wavelength have identical energy and momentum irrespective of radiation intensity, due to consistent photon energy.
text_html:
Frequency and Energy
All light photons of a particular frequency or wavelength have identical energy and momentum irrespective of radiation intensity, due to consistent photon energy.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
text: Photon Intensity Impact An increase in light intensity of a specific wavelength raises the number of photons per second crossing an area, not the energy of individual photons.
text_html:
Photon Intensity Impact
An increase in light intensity of a specific wavelength raises the number of photons per second crossing an area, not the energy of individual photons.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
text: Photon Neutrality Photons are neutrally charged and remain unaffected by electric or magnetic fields.
text_html:
Photon Neutrality
Photons are neutrally charged and remain unaffected by electric or magnetic fields.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
text: Photon-Particle Collisions In collisions involving photons, the total energy and momentum are conserved, but the number of photons may change due to absorption or creation.
text_html:
Photon-Particle Collisions
In collisions involving photons, the total energy and momentum are conserved, but the number of photons may change due to absorption or creation.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Matter Waves-Wave Nature of Particles - De-Broglie Relation
text: Dual Nature of Light The dual wave-particle nature of light becomes evident in many experiments. Wave phenomena: interference, diffraction, and polarisation. Particle phenomena: photoelectric effect and Compton effect.
text_html:
Dual Nature of Light
The dual wave-particle nature of light becomes evident in many experiments.
  • Wave phenomena: interference, diffraction, and polarisation.
  • Particle phenomena: photoelectric effect and Compton effect.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Matter Waves-Wave Nature of Particles - De-Broglie Relation
text: The Role of Experiment The suitability of wave or particle description depends on the nature of the experiment. Both descriptions can be required, for example, when seeing an object with the eye.
text_html:
The Role of Experiment
The suitability of wave or particle description depends on the nature of the experiment. Both descriptions can be required, for example, when seeing an object with the eye.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Matter Waves-Wave Nature of Particles - De-Broglie Relation
text: Eye Mechanism Wave description: Gathering and focusing mechanism of light by the eye-lens. Particle (Photon) description: Absorption by rods and cones of the retina.
text_html:
Eye Mechanism
  • Wave description: Gathering and focusing mechanism of light by the eye-lens.
  • Particle (Photon) description: Absorption by rods and cones of the retina.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Matter Waves-Wave Nature of Particles - De-Broglie Relation
text: De Broglie's Hypothesis Louis Victor de Broglie proposed that particles of matter could exhibit wave-like properties under suitable conditions, suggesting the symmetrical nature of matter and energy.
text_html:
De Broglie's Hypothesis
Louis Victor de Broglie proposed that particles of matter could exhibit wave-like properties under suitable conditions, suggesting the symmetrical nature of matter and energy.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Matter Waves-Wave Nature of Particles - De-Broglie Relation
text: de Broglie Relation The wavelength λ associated with a particle of momentum p is given by λ = h/p = h/mv .
text_html:
de Broglie Relation
The wavelength λ associated with a particle of momentum p is given by
λ = h/p = h/mv.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Matter Waves-Wave Nature of Particles - De-Broglie Relation
text: Planck's Constant Planck's constant h bridges the wave attribute λ and particle attribute p in the de Broglie relation.
text_html:
Planck's Constant
Planck's constant h bridges the wave attribute λ and particle attribute p in the de Broglie relation.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Matter Waves-Wave Nature of Particles - De-Broglie Relation
text: Photons and de Broglie Wavelength For photons, de Broglie wavelength matches the wavelength of the electromagnetic radiation they are associated with, as described by: λ = c/v .
text_html:
Photons and de Broglie Wavelength
For photons, de Broglie wavelength matches the wavelength of the electromagnetic radiation they are associated with, as described by:
λ = c/v.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Matter Waves-Wave Nature of Particles - De-Broglie Relation
text: de Broglie Wavelength and Particle Mass The de Broglie wavelength is smaller for heavier particles or those with higher energy. Example calculation for a macroscopic object like a ball reveals extremely small wavelengths.
text_html:
de Broglie Wavelength and Particle Mass
The de Broglie wavelength is smaller for heavier particles or those with higher energy.
  • Example calculation for a macroscopic object like a ball reveals extremely small wavelengths.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Matter Waves-Wave Nature of Particles - De-Broglie Relation
text: Macroscopic vs. Sub-Atomic Particles Macroscopic objects do not exhibit wave-like properties, while sub-atomic particles' wave character is significant and measurable.
text_html:
Macroscopic vs. Sub-Atomic Particles
Macroscopic objects do not exhibit wave-like properties, while sub-atomic particles' wave character is significant and measurable.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Matter Waves-Wave Nature of Particles - De-Broglie Relation
text: Significance of de Broglie's Hypothesis De Broglie's hypothesis is crucial for understanding the dual nature of matter, validated by experimental observation in the quantum realm.
text_html:
Significance of de Broglie's Hypothesis
De Broglie's hypothesis is crucial for understanding the dual nature of matter, validated by experimental observation in the quantum realm.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Introduction
text: Maxwell's Equations and Wave Nature of Light Maxwell's equations of electromagnetism paired with Hertz's experiments validated the wave nature of light in 1887.
text_html:
Maxwell's Equations and Wave Nature of Light
Maxwell's equations of electromagnetism paired with Hertz's experiments validated the wave nature of light in 1887.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Introduction
text: Discovery in the Discharge Tube Experiments on electric discharge through gases at low pressures led to significant discoveries in atomic structure, including X-rays and electrons.
text_html:
Discovery in the Discharge Tube
Experiments on electric discharge through gases at low pressures led to significant discoveries in atomic structure, including X-rays and electrons.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Introduction
text: Roentgen and X-rays Roentgen discovered X-rays in 1895, furthering the understanding of atomic structure.
text_html:
Roentgen and X-rays
Roentgen discovered X-rays in 1895, furthering the understanding of atomic structure.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Introduction
text: The Discovery of Electron J.J. Thomson identified the electron in 1897 during his research on cathode rays, marking an important milestone in atomic physics.
text_html:
The Discovery of Electron
J.J. Thomson identified the electron in 1897 during his research on cathode rays, marking an important milestone in atomic physics.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Introduction
text: Cathode Rays Cathode rays, streams of fast-moving negatively charged particles, are created when low-pressure gas is exposed to an electric field. They produce a glow on the cathode-side glass.
text_html:
Cathode Rays
Cathode rays, streams of fast-moving negatively charged particles, are created when low-pressure gas is exposed to an electric field. They produce a glow on the cathode-side glass.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Introduction
text: Thomson's Experiment J.J. Thomson used electric and magnetic fields to determine the speed and specific charge (e/m) of cathode ray particles, finding their value independent of the cathode material.
text_html:
Thomson's Experiment
J.J. Thomson used electric and magnetic fields to determine the speed and specific charge (e/m) of cathode ray particles, finding their value independent of the cathode material.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Introduction
text: Speed of Cathode Rays Cathode rays travel at 0.1 to 0.2 times the speed of light, showing their high-speed nature.
text_html:
Speed of Cathode Rays
Cathode rays travel at 0.1 to 0.2 times the speed of light, showing their high-speed nature.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Introduction
text: Universality of Cathode Rays Cathode rays are made of particles identified as electrons, found to be consistent across different materials and gases, demonstrating their universality.
text_html:
Universality of Cathode Rays
Cathode rays are made of particles identified as electrons, found to be consistent across different materials and gases, demonstrating their universality.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Introduction
text: Photoelectric Effect and Electron Emission Ultraviolet light and heating metals to high temperatures both emitted negatively charged particles, found identical to cathode ray particles, confirming them as electrons.
text_html:
Photoelectric Effect and Electron Emission
Ultraviolet light and heating metals to high temperatures both emitted negatively charged particles, found identical to cathode ray particles, confirming them as electrons.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
topic: Introduction
text: Millikan's Oil-Drop Experiment Millikan measured the elementary charge of an electron with high precision, establishing that electric charge is quantized and allowing for the determination of electron mass.
text_html:
Millikan's Oil-Drop Experiment
Millikan measured the elementary charge of an electron with high precision, establishing that electric charge is quantized and allowing for the determination of electron mass.
